source.title: Beyond Borders: Why the Muslim World Needs a Unified Digital Payments System
source.summary: The Fragmentation ProblemWhen a Malaysian entrepreneur wants to purchase textiles from an Indonesian supplier, or when a Saudi charity seeks to transfer funds to a relief organization in Bangladesh,...
source.author: Guest Contributor
source.body_text: When a Malaysian entrepreneur wants to purchase textiles from an Indonesian supplier, or when a Saudi charity seeks to transfer funds to a relief organization in Bangladesh, they encounter a labyrinth of intermediaries, currency conversions, and transaction fees that can consume up to 7% of the transfer value. This inefficiency stands in stark contrast to the seamless digital payment systems emerging within individual nations, and represents a missed opportunity for the 1.8 billion Muslims across 57 Organization of Islamic Cooperation (OIC) member states. The World Bank estimates that remittances to OIC countries exceeded $400 billion in 2023, with intra-OIC trade valued at approximately $600 billion annually. Yet these massive flows remain shackled by fragmented payment infrastructures that rely heavily on Western-dominated networks like SWIFT, Visa, and Mastercard. The average cost of sending remittances within OIC countries stands at 6.8%, nearly double the United Nations Sustainable Development Goal target of 3%. This represents an annual drain of over $27 billion; resources that could otherwise fuel economic development, poverty alleviation, and entrepreneurship across the Muslim world. The case for a unified OIC digital payments infrastructure extends beyond mere economics into the realm of Islamic principle. The Quran explicitly encourages cooperation among believers: "And cooperate in righteousness and piety, but do not cooperate in sin and aggression" (5:2). Economic integration that facilitates halal commerce, reduces exploitation through excessive fees, and strengthens the collective capacity of Muslim nations represents a contemporary application of this divine directive. The Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) established the first Islamic state in Medina with a unified market system that eliminated arbitrary barriers to trade among diverse tribes. His prohibition of najash (artificial price inflation) and his emphasis on transparency in commercial transactions provide timeless principles for modern payment systems. A unified digital infrastructure could embed these Shariah-compliant principles at the architectural level, ensuring that every transaction across the Muslim world adheres to Islamic commercial ethics. Moreover, the Islamic concept of the ummah; the global community of believers; calls for practical solidarity beyond rhetorical…

x.single_post: Fragmented payments cost the Muslim world billions. A unified OIC digital payments system could cut fees, support halal commerce, and deepen economic cooperation. Read the full argument…
thread: The problem: intra-OIC payments face intermediaries, currency conversions and fees that can consume up to 7% of transfer value, despite growing national digital systems.
The scale: remittances to OIC countries topped $400B in 2023; intra-OIC trade is about $600B. High transfer costs—average 6.8%—nearly double the SDG target of 3%.
The case: a unified payments infrastructure could reduce costs, embed Shariah-compliant principles like transparency, and mobilize funds for development and entrepreneurship.
Read the full piece for policy steps and implications. Read: https://islamiceconomics.github.io/blog/2026-02-15-beyond-borders-why-the-muslim-world-needs-a-unified-digital-.html

linkedin.post: Fragmented payment systems are imposing tangible costs across the Muslim world. When entrepreneurs and charities move funds across OIC borders, intermediaries and conversions can consume up to 7% of the value. With remittances to OIC countries exceeding $400 billion in 2023 and intra-OIC trade around $600 billion, the aggregate drain is substantial: the average intra-OIC remittance cost is 6.8%, nearly double the UN SDG target of 3%, representing an annual loss of over $27 billion. This article argues for a unified OIC digital payments infrastructure that could lower transaction costs, embed Shariah-compliant commercial principles (such as transparency and fairness), and reallocate resources toward development, poverty alleviation, and entrepreneurship. The piece situates the case within both economic data and Islamic norms that encourage cooperation and fair commerce. Read the full analysis and policy considerations here: https://islamiceconomics.github.io/blog/2026-02-15-beyond-borders-why-the-muslim-world-needs-a-unified-digital-.html

upscrolled.post:
Cross-border transfers within the OIC face a patchwork of rails, conversions and fees that often reach 6–7% of the transfer value. With remittances and trade in the hundreds of billions, those costs add up and reduce funds available for development.

A regional digital payments infrastructure—designed with Shariah principles of transparency and fairness—could lower costs and improve liquidity between member states. Read the full article at the…

short_video.title: Beyond Borders: A Unified Payments System for the Muslim World
short_video.hook: What if intra-OIC payments cost half as much—and those savings went to businesses and aid?
short_video.voiceover: Cross-border payments across OIC countries remain fragmented. Fees and conversions can eat up to 7% of a transfer. With remittances above $400 billion and intra-OIC trade near $600 billion, that adds up. A unified digital payments system—built with Shariah principles like transparency—could cut costs, boost trade, and free funds for development. Read the full article at the source URL.
